summary:
A placebo effect occurs when a non-active treatment produces beneficial results because the recipient believes it will work. Option C, sham surgery, exemplifies this principle through simulated procedures that provide no tangible medical benefit yet may lead to perceived improvements based solely on patient beliefs about effectiveness. Other options like herbal remedies, physiotherapy, and psychotherapies inherently contain active components beyond just suggestion.
Claim: Sham surgery serves as an example of a placebo.
Evidence: 
- It mimics actual surgical procedures without including the intended interventions.
- Patient expectations significantly influence perceived benefits despite lacking physiological changes.
Answer: C

This summary maintains the core points regarding what constitutes a placebo and why sham surgery qualifies specifically within the context provided. Key aspects such as the nature of sham surgery and its reliance on patient belief are highlighted succinctly.
